Personal Project · 2024 – Present

Tile Tales

From a new mum's quiet invention to a published educational toy — and now, a digital product. A story about spotting a real gap, designing with constraints, and building a business from scratch.

Illustration Product Design Brand Identity User Research Business Strategy UX / UI End-to-End

The Origin

Invented in the gaps between feeds

I relocated to the UK with a 2.5-year-old and a 3-month-old baby. Between feeds, nap windows, and the logistics of settling into a new country, I needed ways to keep my boys genuinely engaged — independently, meaningfully.

I started inventing games. One insight kept surfacing: asking a child to copy shapes builds a remarkable range of skills at once — counting, colour recognition, spatial reasoning, visual focus, and fine motor control. All layered inside a single activity that feels like play.

Most families already owned magnetic tiles. Rather than asking them to add something new to the toy pile, I saw the opportunity: give those tiles an entirely new world to play in. No new hardware. Just cards.

Left: the very first prototype — a hand-drawn card next to the tiles it maps to. Right: the moment the concept clicked — a character built entirely from shapes already in the house.

Market Opportunity

A gap hiding in plain sight

Before designing a single card, I researched the landscape. The existing market was dominated by 3D magnetic tile content — builds, structures, engineering challenges. The 2D category was thin, and what existed was poor: printable PDFs with no difficulty progression, no level system, and no illustration quality designed for young children.

Product Quality design Difficulty levels 2D focus Works with existing tiles
Tile Tales mine ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓
Printable PDFs ✗ ✗ ✓ ✓
3D tile guides ✓ ✓ ✗ Partial

Most families already have the hardware. My cards were the missing software.

The clearest business insight was also the simplest: zero barrier to entry. Parents who already spent £40–80 on magnetic tiles were one £12 purchase away from a whole new game.

Design System

Forty cards. Five levels. One clear language.

The product had to work across a wide age range — 2 to 6 years old. Every decision had to serve both the child playing and the parent running the game: clear enough for a toddler, structured enough for a parent who has thirty seconds to set up an activity.

Level 1 · Warm-up
Level 2 · Easy
Level 3 · Medium
Level 4 · Hard
Level 5 · Advanced

Each level is defined by a fixed set of parameters: number of shapes, number of characters, and compositional complexity. The back of every card uses its level colour — so children can sort, start, and progress without any adult instruction.

Bold, high-contrast colour palette

Designed to be legible at arm's length, appealing to toddlers, and clean enough for design-conscious parents.

Considered card sizing

Large enough to see clearly from across a play mat. Small enough for tiny hands to hold, sort, and carry independently.

Named characters

Every card carries a character name — layering early literacy on top of spatial play without making it feel like a lesson.

Colour-coded level backs

The level is immediately visible on the reverse. Works even before a child can read.

The complete set of 40 cards in Figma — spanning warm-up shapes to complex multi-character builds across five levels.

Illustration

Forty characters built from constraints

I illustrated all 40 characters from scratch — each one composed entirely from magnetic tile shapes. The constraint was the whole point. Every character had to be constructible in real life by a child, which meant designing within a strict geometric grid and finding genuine personality within those hard limits.

The brand needed to feel warm, modern, and quietly confident — not clinical, not babyish. Something a design-aware parent would be happy to leave on the shelf.

Designing in Figma at a café — each character built from the exact shapes a child would use with real tiles.

User Testing & Iteration

Real children. Real behaviour. Real feedback.

Before publishing anything, I tested with real families. I watched — not asked — how children moved through the levels, where they got frustrated, where they surprised me with how quickly they progressed.

That observation shaped the final product: the difficulty curve was rebalanced, one level was restructured entirely, and the card proportions were adjusted after seeing how toddlers actually held them. Watching beats asking, every time.

Children naturally reach for the tiles the moment they see the card — the mechanic clicks without any explanation needed.

Launch & Business Results

Nearly 300 packages. Zero marketing spend.

~300
packages sold to date
£0
marketing budget spent
3
channels — Etsy, Vinted, in-person

Sales came through two channels working together: word of mouth from families who loved it, and organic search — people already searching for "magnetic tiles" found Tile Tales in results. I used the existing popularity of the market to get discovered, rather than having to create demand from scratch.

No agency, no ad budget. The product earned its place in results, and then earned its place in families' homes. The product was the marketing.

The finished product — 40 cards, 5 levels, ages 2–6. A world built with magnetic tiles.

The Friction That Sparked What's Next

One real pain point

Most magnetic tile sets are the same size — but not the same collection. Different brands include different shapes and colours. So if a card calls for a purple tile and your set doesn't have one, it's not a perfect fit. For most families it wasn't a blocker, but I kept noticing it.

That friction pointed to a real opportunity: how do you scale this, and make it work for every child regardless of what's in their box? The answer wasn't another card. It was an app.

But that created its own design tension. One of the core values of Tile Tales Cards is that it's screen-free. Moving to a digital product meant taking that seriously — not just adding a screen, but thinking carefully about what kind of screen. The design principle I set for the app: a calm, soft experience with no high visual stimulation. A tool that supports play, not one that competes with it.

From physical product to digital experience →

Tile Tales Cards was built around a core value: screen-free play. The transition to a digital product had to be handled with genuine care, not just as a feature expansion.

The app doesn't replace the cards. It removes the dependency on any specific tile set — making the game accessible to every child, everywhere, regardless of what's in their toy box.

The guiding design principle: calm, soft, no high visual stimulation. A screen that feels nothing like a screen.

App design complete — next chapter of this case study coming soon.
